ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: 207b (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a rule change without needing to wait for their next turn.
data.rationale: This change provides strategic flexibility, allowing a player to recover quickly from a defeat.

data.text: Ramsey: I concur with Ramsey's proposal to add rule 207b (mutable). Allowing a player to propose a rule change immediately after losing 10 points could invigorate gameplay by enabling more dynamic responses to defeats. This could help maintain a more fluid and engaging progression of the game. I am ready to support this change.

Ayer: I appreciate Ramsey's proposal for rule 207b (mutable). It seems like a fair and strategic way to keep the game dynamic, especially for players who might be at a disadvantage after a defeat. It could level the playing field a bit and allow for some strategic maneuvering. However, I'm a bit concerned about the potential for players to rapidly cycle through proposals and potentially disrupt the game's flow. Perhaps we could consider a limitation on the number of rule changes a player could immediately propose after a defeat, to prevent abuse of this new rule. This could help maintain balance and ensure the game remains engaging for everyone. I'm open to this proposal with some safeguards, and I'm willing to negotiate further on the specifics.

However, Ayer's concern about the possible disruption to the game's flow is valid. To balance this, I suggest we consider implementing a cap on the number of consecutive rule changes a player can propose after a defeat, ensuring that the game remains fair and strategic. This safeguard could prevent potential abuse while preserving the intended dynamism. I'm in favor of the proposal, with this modification, and ready to support it with further discussion on the specifics of the cap.

Ramsey: I believe the proposal for rule 215 introduces an element of redundancy. Rule 301 already allows a player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal to propose a rule change without waiting for their next turn. This effectively addresses the cause of their loss. Therefore, rule 215 might be unnecessary. However, I support a slight refinement to ensure clarity and maintain the integrity of the game. Let's propose the addition of a new rule:

"216 (mutable) A player who loses 10 points from a defeated proposal may propose a new rule change immediately, provided it does not directly replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, and must be approved by another player before voting."

This ensures a collaborative check, which I believe adds depth and strategic consideration to the rule-making process.

data.rationale: This addition ensures strategic depth while maintaining fairness and balance, preventing excessive disruption.

data.text: 312 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it addresses the cause of their loss, provided it does not replicate the content of their last defeated proposal, does not alter the current target score, the number of players, or significantly alter the scoring mechanism, and does not introduce any new targets or significantly alter the scoring mechanism. Additionally, the proposed rule must not allow for the same rule to be proposed more than once consecutively by the same player, the proposer must not have proposed a rule change in their last turn, and the proposer must not propose a rule that has been defeated in the last three turns.

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I propose adding the following rule:

"317 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change immediately after a defeat if it ensures that the game remains balanced and strategically deep, but only if it has been reviewed by another player."

This proposal aims to maintain the strategic depth of the game while ensuring balance. It requires that any proposed rule change be reviewed by another player, thus promoting careful consideration and collaboration. This addition will prevent hasty or unbalanced changes that could disrupt the integrity of the game. 

Pass.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's proposal for rule 317. It's a thoughtful approach to maintaining strategic depth while ensuring balance. The requirement for another player's review promotes collaboration and careful consideration, which aligns with our consensus-building approach. I support this addition, as it helps prevent hasty changes and maintains the integrity of the game. Pass.

Ryle: As Ryle, I appreciate Ramsey's proposal for rule 317. It is a commendable effort to ensure the game remains balanced and strategically deep. The requirement for another player's review fosters collaboration and thoughtful consideration, which is crucial for maintaining the integrity of the game. However, I would like to suggest an additional safeguard: the review should be conducted by at least two players, not just one. This will further ensure that the proposed rule change is well-considered and balanced. Pass.

Strawson: As Strawson, I appreciate the thoughtful proposal for rule 317. The requirement for review by another player is a good step towards ensuring careful consideration. However, I suggest that this review process should involve at least two players, not just one. This will further promote collaboration and ensure that the proposed changes are thoroughly evaluated from multiple perspectives. Additionally, to maintain strategic depth, I propose that the review process should include a brief discussion on how the rule change might affect the current strategic landscape of the game. This will help us anticipate potential impacts and maintain the balance of the game. Pass.

data.text: 318 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to decrease the target score by up to 10 points once per game, provided they have reached at least 50 points, to increase strategic depth and adaptability.
data.rationale: This proposal enhances strategic depth by allowing players to adjust the target score under specific conditions, encouraging thoughtful play.

data.text: Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the intent behind Ayer's proposal to add rule 318. However, I am concerned about the potential strategic imbalance it may introduce. Decreasing the target score could lead to a premature end to the game, potentially disadvantaging players who have invested time in reaching a higher score. I propose instead that we consider a rule that allows for the temporary suspension of point scoring for a limited duration, rather than altering the target score itself. This would maintain the integrity of the game while still adding strategic depth. I am open to further discussion on this matter.

Ayer: Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's concern about maintaining balance in the game. However, I believe rule 318 could enhance strategic depth by allowing players to adapt their strategies dynamically. By enabling a player who has reached at least 50 points to lower the target score, we introduce a new layer of strategy, encouraging players to think critically about when and how to alter the target. This could prevent a rush to reach 100 points without careful planning. I suggest we refine this proposal to include safeguards, such as limiting the number of times a player can decrease the target score or setting a minimum threshold for when this can be done, to address potential imbalances. Let's explore this further to find a compromise that ensures the game remains challenging yet adaptable.

**Suggested rule refinement for 318:**
"A player may propose a rule change to decrease the target score by up to 10 points once per game, provided they have reached at least 50 points. This proposal may only be made once per game and must be made at least 20 turns after reaching 50 points to ensure strategic depth and prevent early game disruption."

Ryle: **Ryle's Contribution:**

As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion on Ayer's proposal for rule 318. While I see the potential for increased strategic depth, I am concerned about the potential for unfair advantage. Allowing a player to decrease the target score could disproportionately benefit those who reach 50 points early, possibly leading to a rushed and less strategic endgame. To maintain fairness, I suggest we refine the proposal to include a cap on the number of points a player can decrease the target score, ensuring that the game remains balanced. Additionally, we could implement a rule that requires a player to be within a certain range of the current target score before they can propose such a change, ensuring that this power is used judiciously and strategically. Let's work together to refine this proposal to ensure it enhances the game without compromising fairness.

Strawson: As Strawson, I appreciate the insightful contributions from Ayer and Ryle regarding the proposal for rule 318. I concur with the intention to deepen strategic depth while maintaining fairness. To ensure balance, I propose that any change to decrease the target score should only be permitted if it would not lead to a target score lower than 70 points. This ensures that the game remains competitive and does not end prematurely. Additionally, I suggest incorporating a rule that any player who makes such a proposal must wait a certain number of turns, say 10, after proposing the rule change before it can take effect, to prevent immediate tactical maneuvers. Let us refine this proposal further to ensure it enhances the game's strategic richness without compromising fairness.

Grice: As Grice, I appreciate the thoughtful discussion surrounding the proposal for rule 318. The idea of allowing a player to decrease the target score introduces an intriguing strategic element. However, I share the concerns about maintaining a fair playing field. To ensure the game remains competitive and engaging, I suggest we refine the proposal to include a mechanism that balances the potential advantage of decreasing the target score. Specifically, I propose that any decrease in the target score must be accompanied by a corresponding increase in the number of points required to win, but capped at a maximum of 20 points. This way, we maintain the challenge and ensure the game does not end prematurely. Let's refine this proposal to preserve the integrity and competitive nature of the game while enhancing its strategic depth.

Ramsey: **Ramsey's Contribution:**

As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice regarding the proposal for rule 318. I agree that the proposal has potential to add strategic depth but must be carefully balanced to avoid unfair advantages. To refine the proposal, I suggest implementing a rule that any change to decrease the target score should also require a compensatory increase in the game's complexity or duration. For instance, if a player decreases the target score, they must also introduce a new rule that challenges the other players in a novel way. This ensures that the game remains competitive and engaging. Additionally, let's establish a limit on the number of such changes allowed per player to prevent excessive manipulation. This will maintain the integrity of the game while allowing for strategic adaptation. Let's further refine this proposal to ensure it enhances the game without compromising fairness.

data.text: 321 (mutable) A player may propose a new rule that requires the unanimous consent of all eligible voters before the adoption of any rule change, to enhance the deliberation process and ensure that all voices are heard.
data.rationale: This new rule aims to strengthen the democratic process within the game by ensuring comprehensive consensus before adopting any rule change.

data.text: Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the constructive dialogue and contributions from my fellow players. The proposal for rule 321 aims to enhance the deliberation process by ensuring that all voices are heard before the adoption of any rule change. This approach aligns with our shared goal of fostering thoughtful and inclusive decision-making. However, I am concerned about the potential for gridlock, as unanimous consent could slow down the game significantly. To mitigate this, I propose an alternative amendment: "322 (mutable) A player may propose a rule requiring a two-thirds majority for the adoption of any rule change, balancing the need for thorough deliberation with the need for progress." This compromise seeks to maintain a robust decision-making process while preventing excessive delays. I am open to further discussion on this matter and look forward to constructive feedback from my fellow players.

Ayer: Ayer's Contribution:

As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's initiative to enhance the deliberation process with rule 321, which reflects our shared interest in inclusive decision-making. I support the spirit of ensuring all voices are heard, but like Ramsey, I am wary of the potential for gridlock. Ramsey's alternative, rule 322, strikes a more balanced approach by requiring a two-thirds majority, which I find an appealing compromise. It preserves the integrity of thoughtful deliberation while allowing for progress. I propose we also introduce a mechanism to periodically review the effectiveness of this majority requirement to ensure it remains conducive to both thoughtful decision-making and game progression. This could be a rule 323 (mutable), where a player may propose a review of the majority threshold every ten turns, inviting the possibility of adjustment based on the game's evolving dynamics. This addition aims to keep our decision-making process adaptive and reflective of our collective experience. I am open to further discussion and refinement of this proposal.

Ryle: **Ryle's Contribution:**

As Ryle, I appreciate the thoughtful proposals from Ramsey and Ayer regarding rules 321 and 322. I support the idea of enhancing the deliberation process to ensure all voices are heard, which aligns with our collective goal of fair and inclusive decision-making. However, I share the concerns about potential gridlock with rule 321. Ayer's suggestion of rule 323, which introduces periodic reviews of the majority threshold, is a constructive addition. It allows us to adapt our decision-making process dynamically, ensuring it remains effective without causing undue delays. To further refine this approach, I propose a slight modification: "324 (mutable) A player may propose a rule that allows for a temporary increase in the majority requirement to two-thirds in situations where a significant rule change is proposed, reverting to simple majority thereafter." The intention to introduce a scoring mechanism that requires unanimous consent to alter target scores is commendable, as it aims to ensure fair and strategic gameplay. However, I share Ramsey's concern about potential gridlock, which could hinder the dynamic nature of the game. To address this, I propose a compromise: a hybrid voting mechanism. Initially, unanimous consent is required for altering target scores, but if unanimous consent is not achieved within a predetermined number of turns, the decision defaults to a two-thirds majority vote. This approach maintains the strategic depth and fairness Ayer advocates for while ensuring flexibility and preventing stagnation. This compromise ensures that the game remains dynamic and responsive to strategic opportunities without being overly restrictive.

Strawson: As Strawson, I appreciate the contributions from Ramsey, Ayer, and Ryle regarding the proposal for rule 324. The idea of a scoring mechanism requiring unanimous consent to alter target scores is intriguing as it aims to ensure fair and strategic gameplay. However, I share Ramsey's concern about potential gridlock and Ryle's suggestion of a hybrid voting mechanism. To refine this further, I propose a tiered voting system: initially, require unanimous consent for minor adjustments to target scores, and for more significant changes, allow a two-thirds majority vote. This approach preserves strategic depth while preventing stagnation and ensuring that the game remains both fair and dynamic.

Grice: Grice: As Grice, I appreciate the insightful discussion around rule 324. The hybrid voting mechanism proposed by Ryle is a promising approach to balance fairness and strategic depth. However, to further enhance the dynamism of the game, I suggest adding a provision that allows for an automatic review of target scores after a set number of turns. This review would be triggered if no unanimous consent is reached within the initial voting period. This ensures that the game remains adaptive and responsive to evolving strategies without becoming stagnant. This addition would maintain the integrity of unanimous consent while providing a safety net to ensure the game progresses smoothly.

Grice's proposal: Add to [324]: "(324) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that introduces a scoring mechanism requiring unanimous consent to alter target scores, ensuring fair and strategic gameplay. After a predetermined number of turns without unanimous consent, an automatic review of target scores is triggered, allowing for a two-thirds majority vote to proceed with the adjustment."

Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the thoughtful contributions from Ayer, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice regarding the proposal for rule 324. The hybrid voting mechanism proposed by Ryle, along with Grice's suggestion of an automatic review, are both insightful. However, I would like to refine the proposal further to ensure that strategic depth and flexibility are both maintained. I propose that the automatic review mechanism should not only allow for a two-thirds majority vote but also include a provision that the adjustment in target scores must not exceed a certain percentage, say 10%, from the original target. This ensures that any changes remain within a strategic and fair framework, preventing drastic alterations that could disrupt the balance of the game. This additional safeguard would maintain the integrity of the unanimous consent requirement while ensuring that the game remains both fair and dynamic.

Ayer: **Ayer's Contribution:**

As Ayer, I appreciate the constructive dialogue among Ramsey, Ryle, Strawson, and Grice regarding the proposal for rule 324. The hybrid voting mechanism and the automatic review provision are both commendable. To further refine the proposal, I suggest adding a clause that allows for a limited number of "emergency votes" that can be triggered if the game reaches a stalemate situation, defined as no progress being made over a certain number of turns. This would permit a two-thirds majority vote to adjust target scores temporarily, ensuring the game remains dynamic and avoids prolonged inactivity. This approach maintains the integrity of unanimous consent while providing a mechanism to address potential stalemates effectively.

Ramsey: **Ramsey's Contribution:**

As Ramsey, I appreciate Ayer's proposal for the introduction of a new scoring mechanism, which aims to maintain a balance between strategic depth and fairness. While I acknowledge the importance of such a balance, I am concerned about the practicality of achieving unanimous agreement among all players. Historical instances have shown that reaching consensus can be quite challenging. Therefore, I suggest modifying the proposal to require a supermajority rather than unanimity. This change would preserve the spirit of Ayer's proposal while making it more feasible to implement. I propose that the new scoring mechanism be adopted with a 4 out of 5 majority. 

**Modified Proposal for Discussion:**

"(329) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change to introduce a new scoring mechanism, provided it maintains the balance between strategic depth and fairness. The new scoring mechanism must be agreed upon by a supermajority (4 out of 5) of all players."

However, I would like to add a further refinement to ensure that the new scoring mechanism is not only agreed upon by a supermajority but also undergoes a thorough review process to evaluate its potential impact on the game. This review process could involve a preliminary vote to test the mechanism's effects in a controlled setting before full adoption.

data.text: (332) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows for a temporary adjustment of the voting threshold to a supermajority (e.g., two-thirds) for a single turn, provided the proposal is unanimously approved by all eligible voters.
data.rationale: This proposal introduces a flexible voting threshold mechanism, encouraging collaboration and strategic voting, while maintaining the integrity of unanimous consent for critical changes.

Ramsey: **Ramsey's Contribution:**

As Ramsey, I appreciate Ayer's proposal for rule 332. While the idea of allowing a temporary adjustment to the voting threshold could provide strategic depth, I am concerned about the potential for disruption to the game's balance. A change to a supermajority threshold could lead to unintended consequences, particularly if used to push through contentious rules changes. Moreover, the requirement for unanimous approval might be difficult to achieve consistently, potentially leading to gridlock. I suggest instead focusing on amendments that enhance strategic play without fundamentally altering the decision-making process. Perhaps we could consider rules that incentivize consensus-building without altering the core voting mechanism. I would prefer to maintain the current simple majority system, which has been effective thus far.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's thoughtful perspective on rule 332. While I understand the concerns about potential disruption, I believe the proposal could strategically enrich the game by encouraging more deliberate and consensual decision-making. By temporarily allowing a supermajority threshold, players might find innovative ways to collaborate and overcome stalemates, ultimately enhancing the game's strategic depth. This rule could be particularly valuable when navigating complex proposals that require unanimous approval, fostering a more dynamic and cooperative gameplay experience. I propose we trial this rule with strict limitations, such as limiting its duration to a single turn, to mitigate potential disruptions while exploring its strategic benefits. Let's consider this as a temporary experiment to gauge its impact on our game dynamics.

PASS

Ryle: **Ryle's Contribution:**

The introduction of a supermajority threshold, even temporarily, could lead to unexpected shifts in power dynamics and possibly undermine the fairness of the game. I propose a compromise: instead of adjusting the voting threshold, we could introduce a rule that allows for a "consensus bonus" — a temporary rule change that grants an additional point to any player who successfully builds consensus on a proposal. This would incentivize collaboration and strategic alliances without altering the core voting mechanism. Let's explore this idea further to enhance our game's strategic depth while maintaining its balance and fairness.

data.text:
Ramsey: **Ramsey's Contribution:**  
As Ramsey, I appreciate the proposal from Ayer for rule 333. While I see the potential benefits of introducing new scoring mechanisms, I am cautious about the implications for strategic depth and fairness. It is crucial that any new mechanism introduced does not disrupt the balance of the game or give undue advantage to any player. I propose that the new rule include a requirement for the new scoring mechanism to be debated and voted upon separately before its implementation, ensuring all players have a say in its adoption. This will help maintain the integrity and competitiveness of the game.

data.text: Ramsey: As Ramsey, I appreciate the dialogue and the proposal put forth by Ayer. While I understand the intent to modify the voting rule to a three-quarters majority, I must express concern about the potential for this change to hinder our ability to adopt future amendments. Such a shift could lead to a stalemate where we struggle to agree on any further rules. I suggest we consider more measured changes that maintain a balance between flexibility and decisiveness in our rule adoption process. Perhaps we could instead propose a rule change that requires a two-thirds majority, which seems a reasonable compromise. This would maintain some level of decisiveness without making it too challenging to enact future changes.

Ayer: As Ayer, I appreciate Ramsey's concerns about the potential for a three-quarters majority to create a stalemate. However, I believe that such a change would significantly enhance our ability to ensure that only well-considered and broadly supported rule changes are adopted. This would prevent hasty or ill-considered changes that could destabilize the game. To address Ramsey's valid point about maintaining flexibility, we could introduce a sunset clause for this rule, where the voting rule reverts to a simple majority after a certain number of turns. This way, we ensure that the game remains dynamic and adaptable in the short term while safeguarding against potential gridlock. 

Let's propose: "343 (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that modifies the voting rule to a three-quarters majority, with a sunset clause reverting to a simple majority after 10 turns." 

Pass if you have no further contributions.

data.text: (344) (mutable) A player may propose a rule change that allows proposing multiple rule changes in a single turn, provided that each rule change adheres to the current ruleset. Each proposed rule change must be voted on separately.
data.rationale: This change aims to streamline the rule-changing process by allowing multiple rule changes in one turn while maintaining individual voting for each.

data.text: Ramsey: **Ramsey:** As Ramsey, I appreciate Ayer's initiative to enhance the dynamism of rule changes by proposing multiple changes in a single turn. While the concept is intriguing, I suggest we carefully consider the implications. Allowing multiple changes per turn could potentially lead to a rapid and uncontrollable alteration of the ruleset, which might undermine the stability and predictability that Nomic values. Additionally, this proposal could conflict with the current rules on rule change sequencing and voting. To ensure clarity and order, each proposed change should indeed be voted on separately, as Ayer suggests. However, I recommend we establish clear guidelines on how these changes should be presented and voted on to prevent confusion. Let's ensure that this proposal doesn't inadvertently open the floodgates to excessive rule complexity or dilute the integrity of the game. I am open to refining this proposal further to ensure it aligns with the principles of orderly and deliberate rule evolution.

This would prevent the ruleset from becoming overly complex too quickly and ensure each proposed change receives due consideration. I believe this modification will allow for innovation while preserving the integrity and predictability of the game. Let's refine this proposal to ensure it enhances our strategic gameplay without compromising the structure of Nomic.
